summary:
The Rise of Napoleon Bonaparte (1795–1799 AD) was one of history's most extraordinary ascents — from Corsican-born artillery officer to Emperor of the French within a decade, made possible by the unique circumstances of the [[event:107|French Revolution]], which simultaneously destroyed the old aristocratic hierarchy that had blocked merit-based advancement and created the military and political vacuum that ambition and genius could fill. Napoleon rose to prominence through spectacular military success: the [[event:3316|Italian Campaign]] of 1796–1797, where he transformed a demoralized French army into a war-winning force through rapid maneuver, determined aggression, and personal leadership, defeating Austrian and Piedmontese forces in a series of brilliant engagements and acquiring a celebrity that rivaled the Revolution's political leaders. His [[event:3317|Egyptian Campaign]] (1798–1799), though militarily inconclusive, added an aura of exotic ambition and was accompanied by the scientific expedition that produced the Description de l'Égypte.

Napoleon's seizure of power — the coup of 18 Brumaire (November 9, 1799), in which he and his allies overthrew the Directory and established the Consulate with himself as First Consul — was both audacious and revealing. The Directory had been chronically unstable and unpopular; many French citizens welcomed a strong, decisive leader after a decade of revolutionary upheaval. Napoleon positioned himself as the embodiment of the Revolution's achievements (legal equality, meritocracy, religious tolerance, rational administration) while ending its instability and violence. The Consulate quickly stabilized France domestically — the Napoleonic Code (1804), the Concordat with the Pope, and administrative centralization were its domestic achievements — while Napoleon's military dominance of Europe would define the first fifteen years of the 19th century.
